behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This is a backoff calculation rule on attempt count and cap, which can fail entirely within one function/object boundary. Rejected Integration/System because the behavior text only specifies the formula ("doubles... up to a configured maximum"), not cross-module handoff or real HTTP/subprocess effects.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure backoff calculator (or retry-state object) with consecutive failure counts and assert delays follow 1x, 2x, 4x... and clamp at max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Failure is at module boundaries: response classification + retry policy + delay computation. A unit test of only parser or only backoff misses wiring bugs. Rejected System because a stubbed HTTP response object/status (e.g., 429 + headers) reproduces the failure without real network.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry middleware/service with a fake transport returning rate-limit responses; assert it classifies as retryable and computes expected delay from configured policy/header values.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This checks orchestration across timer/wait handling, request replay, and result propagation. Rejected System because real HTTP is not required to expose replay/wiring bugs; fake timers plus stub transport can verify resend payload and returned value.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake clock and stub transport: first response is rate-limited, second is success. Assert one waited retry occurs, original request is resent unchanged, and caller receives final success without extra handling.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Typed error outcome depends on interaction between retry counter exhaustion and error mapping. Rejected Unit because mapping-only tests miss counter/wiring defects; rejected System because stubbed repeated rate-limit failures can trigger the same path without real network.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries, stub transport to always return rate-limit failure, run `call()`, and assert rejection is `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected metadata instead of generic error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This is a multi-module state-machine termination property (retry loop + quota state), not a single-function check. Rejected System because infinite-loop risk is in local control flow and is reproducible with deterministic stub failures and fake time; no real OS/network dependency is required.
behaviors.test_description: With fake timers and always-rate-limited stub responses, execute `call()` and assert attempts stop at configured bound, promise settles with expected error, and no additional retries are scheduled.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: The failure appears when concurrent callers interact through shared in-process state; unit tests of quota math alone can miss cross-caller coordination races. Rejected System because behavior text explicitly scopes to "same process" and can be validated with in-process async concurrency and stub transport.
behaviors.test_description: Start multiple concurrent `call()` invocations against one client/account context, drive deterministic stub responses, and assert quota consumption is globally shared across callers (not per-caller isolated).

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Queue ordering bugs occur at the boundary between submit path, waiting queue, and dispatcher. Rejected Unit because a queue helper alone may pass while integration wiring reorders dispatch; rejected System because ordering can be proven with in-process scheduler/transport doubles.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue several blocked requests, then release capacity and verify dispatch sequence matches submission order (FIFO) under async contention.
